title: Zhipu founder Tang Jie tells staff frontier AI should stay open, contradicting Beijing's export talks
subtitle: In an internal memo titled 'The Giant Wave Has Arrived,' the co-founder of the Chinese lab behind GLM argued security comes from broad participation, not walls — a rare public dissent from a proposed national policy.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Tang Jie, co-founder and chief scientist of Beijing-based Zhipu AI — which operates internationally under the Z.ai brand and trades on the Hong Kong exchange as ticker 2513 — circulated an internal memo to staff titled 'The Giant Wave Has Arrived' arguing that frontier artificial general intelligence should remain open and accessible to everyone. Genuine security, he wrote, comes from broad participation, sharing, and oversight rather than technological barriers. Bloomberg reviewed the memo and published excerpts on July 12; the note was clearly written for staff but designed to leak.

The timing is what makes it news. On July 7, Reuters reported that China's Ministry of Commerce had held closed-door meetings with Alibaba, ByteDance, and Zhipu about limiting overseas access to China's most advanced open-weight and closed-weight AI models — the Chinese equivalent of a U.S. export control regime for frontier models. Zhipu's founder is now publicly disagreeing with a policy his own government is drafting, from a company whose flagship GLM-series models are among the most-downloaded Chinese open-weight releases outside China. That is unusual.

The broader question the memo raises is which strategic school wins in Beijing. One camp treats frontier AI as a national-security asset to be gated, tracked, and licensed for export — the same instinct the U.S. applied to semiconductor manufacturing tools. The other treats openness as a competitive weapon, arguing that Chinese labs' global influence depends on developers downloading Qwen and GLM to build with. Tang Jie is publicly picking a side, at some real risk, and pointing out that if China restricts its open models, the developer ecosystem those models built will simply move on to Llama and Mistral.

A note for learners: 'open source' in AI is not a purely technical or ideological choice — it is an industrial policy lever, and different governments are pulling it in opposite directions right now. The U.S. is more restrictive on frontier weights than a year ago; parts of Europe are pushing for open by default; and China is now debating internally. If you build on open models today, keep a close eye on where the weights actually live and under what license, because the terms could change with a single policy announcement.

sources.title: Zhipu's Chinese Founder Says Frontier AI Should Stay Open to All — Bloomberg
